claim: The Currier A/B scribal split represents two different scribes encoding the same underlying language using two distinct but related glyph substitution tables (i.e., a two-key polyalphabetic or two-alphabet substitution), such that the same plaintext phoneme maps to different glyphs in A vs. B â€” testable by checking whether word-length distributions and initial/final glyph frequencies in A and B are statistically compatible with a common underlying vocabulary after glyph remapping.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) differ in glyph frequencies but share structural constraints
The Zipf exponent (0.8946) across the full corpus suggests a unified underlying lexical distribution
Belief [0.668] already rates this hypothesis highly based on prior evidence
evidence_against: A/B split could reflect two different dialects or languages rather than two cipher keys on one language
If two scribes used different keys, overlapping sections should show frequency discontinuities at folio boundaries â€” not yet confirmed
test: Extract the top-50 most frequent words in Currier A and top-50 in Currier B; compute Jaccard similarity of these sets and compare to the expected overlap if A and B are random draws from the same vocabulary. Separately, test whether the word-length frequency distribution of A and B are drawn from the same distribution (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test).
test_metric: Jaccard similarity of top-50 word sets (A vs. B); KS statistic on word-length distributions
test_threshold: Jaccard >0.3 and KS p-value >0.05 (fail to reject same distribution) supports shared vocabulary; Jaccard <0.15 or KS p<0.01 supports two distinct languages

tests_run.test: extract the top-50 most frequent words in currier a and top-50 in currier b; compute jaccard similar
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: Distinguishing 'same language, two keys' from 'two languages' is critical: the former allows cross-dialect frequency alignment to bootstrap decipherment, the latter requires independent attacks on each section
